Security Policies.cmd: show running security-policy
Security Policies.desc: Currently active security policy rules as enforced by the dataplane

Security Policies.cmd: test security-policy-match from <zone> to <zone> source <ip> destination <ip> application <app>
Security Policies.desc: Simulates traffic and shows which rule would match

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> from <source-zone>
Security Policies.desc: Specifies source security zone for the rule
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web from trust
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> to <destination-zone>
Security Policies.desc: Specifies destination security zone for the rule
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web to untrust
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> source <source-address>
Security Policies.desc: Restricts rule to traffic from a specific source address
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web source Workstations
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> destination <destination-address>
Security Policies.desc: Restricts rule to traffic destined for a specific address
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web destination any
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> application <application-name>
Security Policies.desc: Matches rule to a specific App-ID application
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web application [ web-browsing ssl ]
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> action allow/deny/drop
Security Policies.desc: Sets action: allow, deny (TCP reset), or drop (silent discard)
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web action allow
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> log-start yes
Security Policies.desc: Enables logging at the start of matching sessions
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web log-start yes
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> log-end yes
Security Policies.desc: Enables logging at the end of matching sessions
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web log-end yes
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> disabled yes/no
Security Policies.desc: Enables or disables the rule without deleting it
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Old-Rule disabled yes
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> profile-setting profiles virus <av-profile>
Security Policies.desc: Attaches an Antivirus profile to the rule
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web profile-setting profiles virus default
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> profile-setting profiles vulnerability <vp-profile>
Security Policies.desc: Attaches a Vulnerability Protection profile to the rule
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web profile-setting profiles vulnerability strict
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> profile-setting profiles url-filtering <url-profile>
Security Policies.desc: Attaches a URL Filtering profile to the rule
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web profile-setting profiles url-filtering default
[edit]

Security Policies.cmd: set rulebase security rules <rule-name> tag [ <tag1> <tag2> ]
Security Policies.desc: Tags a security rule for grouping and policy filtering
Security Policies.example:
# (configure) set rulebase security rules Allow-Web tag [ Production WebTraffic ]
[edit]

NAT.cmd: set rulebase nat rules <rule-name> source-translation static-ip bi-directional yes translated-address <ip>

NAT.cmd: set rulebase nat rules <rule-name> destination-translation translated-address <ip>
NAT.desc: Destination NAT to redirect inbound traffic to an internal IP
NAT.example:

[edit]

NAT.cmd: set rulebase nat rules <rule-name> destination-translation translated-port <port>
NAT.desc: Override destination port for port forwarding scenarios
NAT.example:
# (configure) set rulebase nat rules Inbound-Web destination-translation translated-port 8080
[edit]

Commit & Config.desc: Displays the currently active running configuration
Commits only the changes made by a specific administrator
Commit & Config.example: Partial commit job enqueued (admin=jbloggs only). Job ID 145.

Commit & Config.cmd: load config partial from <filename> from-xpath <xpath> to-xpath <xpath>
Commit & Config.desc: Merges a subset of a saved config into the candidate config
Commit & Config.example: Partial config loaded from saved-2026-05-23.xml.  Run 'commit' to activate.

Commit & Config.desc: Displays the most recently committed saved configuration
Commit even when no operator has made changes (useful post-upgrade)
Commit & Config.example: Forcing commit with errors ignored. Job ID 146.
Commit lock added by admin. Reason: "Change window 09:00-10:00".

Commit & Config.desc: Diff between candidate and running config
Commit & Config.example:
Difference between candidate and running configurations:
+ rulebase.security.rules.entry[@name='Allow-Web']
-   action: drop
+   action: allow
+   profile-setting.profiles.virus: default
Commit job enqueued (description: "Add Allow-Web for new subnet"). Job ID 147.

Commit & Config.desc: Commit only changes owned by a specific admin
Commit & Config.example: Partial commit job enqueued (admin=asmith only). Job ID 148.

Commit & Config.desc: Who currently holds a commit lock
Commit & Config.example:
Type    Locked-by   Time                  Comment
commit  admin       2026/05/24 09:00:00   "Change window 09:00-10:00"

Commit & Config.desc: Factory-resets all private configuration data — admin credentials, certificates, private keys, and logs — while preserving the base OS image. Required before RMA or resale. IRREVERSIBLE
Commit & Config.example:
Warning: This operation will permanently delete all private data including
  configurations, administrator accounts, certificates, and logs.
  The device will reboot and return to factory defaults.
  Type "yes" to confirm: yes

Private data reset initiated. System will reboot momentarily.

User-ID.desc: Complete current table of IP address to username mappings
User-ID.example:
IP              Vsys   From                User                       IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD                  ACME\jbloggs                  1582            10800
10.0.2.99       vsys1  Captive-Portal      ACME\contractor1              298             10800
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP                  ACME\asmith                   3204            10800

User-ID.desc: Looks up which username is currently mapped to a specific IP
User-ID.example:
IP: 10.0.2.55
User                     : ACME\jbloggs
Source                   : AD (Active Directory)
Mapped                   : 2026/05/24 08:11:12
Idle timeout remaining   : 1582 sec

User-ID.desc: Connection statistics and event counts for all User-ID agents
User-ID.example:
Agent: AD-Agent-01 (10.0.0.50)
  Status               : connected
  IP-User mappings     : 2,128
  Groups in cache      : 184
  Last update          : 32 sec ago
  Bytes RX / TX        : 14.2 MB / 1.8 MB

User-ID.desc: Configures a connection to a User-ID agent
User-ID.example:
# (configure) set user-id-agent AD-Agent-01 host 10.0.0.50 port 5007
[edit]

User-ID.desc: Resets the User-ID subsystem and clears all mappings — use with caution
User-ID.example: User-ID daemon reset. All cached mappings dropped. Mappings will repopulate from agents.

User-ID.desc: Lists all members of an AD/LDAP group as known to User-ID
User-ID.example:
Group: cn=Domain Users,cn=Users,dc=acme,dc=local
Member count: 1842
Members:
  ACME\jbloggs
  ACME\asmith
  ...

User-ID.desc: Manually removes a user's IP-to-username mapping from the table
User-ID.example: User ACME\jbloggs logged out from vsys1. IP-User mapping removed.

User-ID.desc: Every known username → IP mapping
User-ID.example:
Vsys    User                Group memberships  Mappings
vsys1   ACME\jbloggs        12                 1 (10.0.2.55)
vsys1   ACME\asmith         12                 1 (10.0.2.100)

User-ID.desc: Who is this IP mapped to right now
User-ID.example:
IP             : 10.0.2.55
User           : ACME\jbloggs
Source         : AD
Idle timeout   : 1582 sec remaining

User-ID.desc: Filter the IP-to-user mapping table by mapping source (which User-ID mechanism learned it).
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping all type GP

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP     ACME\asmith         3204            10800

# <type> values: UIA (Windows User-ID agent), UID (PAN-OS integrated agent),
# GP (GlobalProtect), XMLAPI, CP (Authentication/Captive Portal), SSO,
# SYSLOG, TS (Terminal Server agent), RADIUS, EDIR, NTLM, UNKNOWN.

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-user-mapping all option count
User-ID.desc: Return just the total number of active IP-to-user mappings (no table) — quick capacity/health check against the platform limit.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping all option count

Total: 1452 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: Show the IP address(es) a specific user is currently mapped to.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping user acme\jbloggs

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD     ACME\jbloggs        1582            10800

User-ID.desc: All current mappings within a given NetBIOS / AD domain.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping domain ACME

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD     ACME\jbloggs        1582            10800
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP     ACME\asmith         3204            10800

User-ID.desc: Same IP-to-user table but read from the management-plane User-ID process (idmgr). Compare with the dataplane copy (show user ip-user-mapping all) to spot MP-to-DP sync gaps.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all

IP              Vsys   From                User                       IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD                  ACME\jbloggs               1582            10800
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP                  ACME\asmith                3204            10800

# The MP copy is the master that idmgr distributes to the dataplane;
# policy enforcement uses the DP copy. The two should match.

User-ID.desc: Management-plane lookup for a single IP. Handy when the dataplane shows no mapping but the MP does — points to a MP-to-DP distribution problem rather than a learning problem.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp ip 10.0.2.55

IP             : 10.0.2.55
User           : ACME\jbloggs
From           : AD
Idle timeout   : 1582 sec remaining

User-ID.desc: Management-plane mapping table filtered by source type.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all type XMLAPI

IP              Vsys   From    User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.77       vsys1  XMLAPI  ACME\svc-api        600             3600

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-user-mapping-mp all option count
User-ID.desc: Total mapping count on the management plane — compare with the dataplane count to confirm the two planes are in sync.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all option count

Total: 1452 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: IP + source-port-range to user mappings from the Terminal Server (TS) Agent. Used for multi-user hosts (Citrix / RDS) where many users share one IP and are told apart by source port.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping all

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser03         20400       20599

# Each user on the shared TS host gets a unique source-port block; the
# firewall maps a session's source port to the correct user.

User-ID.desc: All port-range-to-user allocations for one Terminal Server IP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping ip 10.4.5.6

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-port-user-mapping all option count
User-ID.desc: Total number of active IP-port-user (Terminal Server) mappings.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping all option count

Total: 240 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: Management-plane copy of the Terminal Server IP-port-user table. Compare against the dataplane to confirm TS-agent mappings have been pushed to the DP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping-mp all

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399

User-ID.desc: Management-plane port-range-to-user allocations for one Terminal Server IP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping-mp ip 10.4.5.6

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199

User-ID.example:

Authentication & Admin.cmd: set shared server-profile radius <name> server <s> host <ip> port 1812 secret <psk>
Authentication & Admin.desc: Define a RADIUS server-profile (host, port, shared secret)

priv:

pub:

NIST CURVE: P-384

# secp384r1 (P-384) offers 192-bit security — suitable for TLS 1.3 key exchange.

  -subj "/C=GB/ST=London/O=MyOrg/CN=internal.example.com"

Generating a RSA private key

writing new private key to 'key.pem'

notAfter=Jun 15 09:00:00 2027 GMT

openssl verify -CAfile root-ca.crt -untrusted intermediate.crt cert.crt
cert.crt: OK

---

-----END CERTIFICATE-----

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect and validate the certificate against a specific CA bundle
Force TLS 1.2 only for the connection test
TLS Client Testing.example:
# If the server supports TLS 1.2 you see:
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2
    Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384

# If TLS 1.2 is disabled server-side:
CONNECTED(00000003)
write:errno=104
# or: alert handshake failure

TLS Client Testing.desc: Force TLS 1.3 only for the connection test
TLS Client Testing.example:
# Server accepts TLS 1.3:
CONNECTED(00000003)
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384

# Server does not support TLS 1.3 (legacy server):
CONNECTED(00000003)
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2   ← negotiated down
# Or: handshake failure if server is strict

Request OCSP stapling status information during the TLS handshake

...
OCSP response:
======================================
OCSP Response Data:
    OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
    Response Type: Basic OCSP Response
    Cert Status: Good
    This Update: Jun 14 08:00:00 2026 GMT
    Next Update: Jun 21 08:00:00 2026 GMT
======================================
# If no OCSP staple is returned: OCSP response: no response sent

TLS Client Testing.desc: Check certificate expiry dates directly from a live TLS endpoint
Test TLS on SMTP using STARTTLS negotiation

220 mail.example.com ESMTP
EHLO openssl.client
250-mail.example.com
250 STARTTLS
STARTTLS
220 Ready to start TLS
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2
    Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
# Also works with: -starttls ftp | imap | ldap | pop3 | xmpp

TLS Client Testing.desc: Test that only TLS 1.2+ is accepted by the server
TLS Client Testing.example:
# If server correctly rejects old protocols:

SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2   (or TLSv1.3)

# Verifying TLS 1.0/1.1 are truly disabled:
openssl s_client -connect hostname:443 -tls1_1
  → handshake failure  ✓ good
openssl s_client -connect hostname:443 -tls1
  → handshake failure  ✓ good

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Print each TLS protocol message direction + type as it flies by
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -msg
>>> TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 0125], ClientHello
    0300  01 21 03 03 ...
<<< TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 007a], ServerHello
    0300  02 76 03 03 ...
<<< TLS 1.3 ChangeCipherSpec [length 0001]
    14 03 03 00 01 01
<<< TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 00xx], EncryptedExtensions
<<< TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 03xx], Certificate
<<< TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 0xxx], CertificateVerify
<<< TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 0030], Finished
>>> TLS 1.3 ChangeCipherSpec [length 0001]
>>> TLS 1.3 Handshake [length 0030], Finished
---
Protocol  : TLSv1.3
Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
# Each >>> = outbound (client); <<< = inbound (server).
# Use -debug for hex dumps of every byte, or -trace for parsed extensions.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Hex-dump every wire byte — very noisy but shows true framing
Full structured TLS message trace (extensions, certs, key_shares)

Hashing & HMAC.example:
openssl dgst -sha256 /path/to/firmware.bin
SHA2-256(/path/to/firmware.bin)= e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

# Compare against a published checksum:
echo "e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  firmware.bin" | sha256sum -c
firmware.bin: OK

# HMAC with a secret key:
openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "mysecretkey" file.txt
HMAC-SHA2-256(file.txt)= 4c1f6b2a9d3e8f7a5b0c2d1e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2

Compute the SHA-512 digest of a file
Compute the MD5 digest of a file (legacy use only — not for security)

Hashing & HMAC.cmd: openssl mac -macopt hexkey:00112233 -macopt digest:SHA256 HMAC < file.txt

Hashing & HMAC.desc: Binary SHA-256 re-encoded as base64 (e.g. for SRI integrity attributes)

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Base64-encode a binary file to a text file
Base64-decode a text file back to binary
Encrypt a file using AES-256-CBC with a password
Decrypt an AES-256-CBC encrypted file using a password
Explicit KDF parameters — portable between boxes

Digital Signatures.desc: Digitally sign a file using an RSA private key
Digital Signatures.example:
# Sign a file
openssl dgst -sha256 -sign private.key -out sig.bin file.txt
# (no output on success — sig.bin is created)

# Verify the signature (requires the corresponding public key)
openssl dgst -sha256 -verify public.key -signature sig.bin file.txt
Verified OK

# If tampered:
openssl dgst -sha256 -verify public.key -signature sig.bin modified_file.txt
Verification Failure

# Extract public key from private key first if needed:
openssl rsa -in private.key -pubout -out public.key

Digital Signatures.desc: Verify a digital signature against a file using the public key

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey private.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin -pkeyopt digest:sha256
openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey rsa.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin -pkeyopt rsa_padding_mode:pss -pkeyopt rsa_pss_saltlen:digest
Digital Signatures.desc: RSA-PSS signature (required for TLS 1.3 with RSA)

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl cms -sign -in file.txt -signer cert.crt -inkey private.key -out signed.p7s -outform DER
openssl cms -verify -in signed.p7s -inform DER -content file.txt -CAfile ca.crt

Performance & Benchmarks.desc: Benchmark AES-256-GCM via EVP (uses AES-NI if available)
Performance & Benchmarks.example:
openssl speed -evp aes-256-gcm
Doing AES-256-GCM for 3s on 16 size blocks: 92345678 AES-256-GCM's in 3.00s
Doing AES-256-GCM for 3s on 64 size blocks: 28901234 AES-256-GCM's in 3.00s
Doing AES-256-GCM for 3s on 256 size blocks: 8345678 AES-256-GCM's in 3.00s
Doing AES-256-GCM for 3s on 1024 size blocks: 2234567 AES-256-GCM's in 3.00s
Doing AES-256-GCM for 3s on 8192 size blocks: 289012 AES-256-GCM's in 3.00s

type              16 bytes   64 bytes  256 bytes 1024 bytes 8192 bytes
aes-256-gcm    492,777.5k  615,738.6k  711,509.3k  763,076.6k  788,635.6k

# Values well above 500 MB/s indicate AES-NI hardware acceleration is active.
# Check: openssl speed -evp aes-256-gcm vs openssl speed aes-256-cbc
#        GCM >> CBC confirms AES-NI is used for authenticated encryption.

GUEST-WIFI                   1160    GUEST-ACL
AUTH-FAILED                  1168    None (central-dhcp)

Show & Status.desc: AP join profile detail including management credentials, CAPWAP timers, and SSH/Telnet access settings.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ap profile name default-ap-profile detailed | begin Management
Management Settings
  AP Username (Plain Text)   : admin
  AP Username (Encrypted)    : 9$abc123...
  AP Password (Encrypted)    : 7$def456...
  AP Secret (Encrypted)      : 7$ghi789...
CAPWAP Settings
  CAPWAP Heartbeat Timer (s) : 30
  CAPWAP Discovery Timer (s) : 10
  CAPWAP Retransmit Count    : 5
  CAPWAP Retransmit Interval : 3
SSH/Telnet
  SSH Access                : Enabled
  Telnet Access             : Disabled

Show & Status.desc: Per-AP tag assignment: site tag, policy tag, RF tag, plus any misconfiguration flag.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ap tag summary
AP Name        AP Mac          Site Tag Name  Policy Tag Name  RF Tag Name  Misconfigured  Tag Source
AP-OFFICE-01   cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1       POLICY-FL1       RF-DEFAULT   No             Static

Show & Status.desc: Hardware model, serial number, regulatory domain. Use to confirm -E (EU/UK) vs -A (NA) variant for 6GHz capability.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ap inventory AP-OFFICE-01
NAME: "AP-OFFICE-01", DESCR: "Cisco Aironet CW9166I"
PID: CW9166I-ROW,  VID: V01,  SN: KWC2306G0F1
Number of Slots in this AP: 3
AP Country code                     : GB
AP Regulatory Domain                : -E

Show & Status.desc: Lists APs in reduced-power operation due to insufficient PoE.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ap power-save summary
Number of APs in Power Save: 1
AP Name           Mode               Reason
AP-WHS-03         Reduced            PoE class 3 (15.4W)
# AP requested Class 6+ (60W) but switch only granted Class 3.
# Result: AP runs with fewer chains, lower TX power.

Show & Status.desc: Global band state. Enabled means the band is in service controller-wide.
BSSIDs each WLAN is broadcasting on a specific AP's radio. If empty, no WLAN is being deployed — usually a tag, policy, or flex-profile mismatch.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 wlan dot11 5ghz
Slot id  : 1
  WLAN ID    BSSID
  1          70bd.9685.9c8f

Show & Status.desc: Full client session detail: auth method, VLAN and source (AAA-override vs policy profile), session timers.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show wireless client mac-address aabb.ccdd.eeff detail
Client MAC Address : aabb.ccdd.eeff
AP Name             : AP-OFFICE-01
AP slot              : 1 (5GHz)
Client Username     : jdoe@acme.com
AP Mac Address      : 70bd.9685.9c80
AP Name             : AP-OFFICE-01
WLAN Profile Name   : CORP-WLAN
Wireless LAN Id     : 1
VLAN                 : 1110 (CORP-FL1)
VLAN Source         : AAA Override (Tunnel-Private-Group-Id)
Client State        : Associated
Policy Manager State: Run
Authentication Algorithm : Open System
AKM                  : 802.1X SHA256
Encryption          : CCMP-128
Session Timeout     : 7200
Session Manager:
  Method            : Dot1x
  Authentication-Status: Authz Success

Show & Status.cmd: show wireless client mac-address <mac> mobility history
Show & Status.desc: Roaming history for a client — which APs it has been on, when, and roam type.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show wireless client mac-address aabb.ccdd.eeff mobility history
Client MAC Address     : aabb.ccdd.eeff
Client IP Address      : 10.11.1.45
Username              : jdoe@acme.com

Roam History:
Time                       AP Name        BSSID            Roam Reason
14:30:01 Apr 21 2026       AP-OFFICE-01   70bd.9685.9c8f   Initial
14:45:23 Apr 21 2026       AP-OFFICE-02   70bd.9685.9c9f   Better signal
15:12:55 Apr 21 2026       AP-OFFICE-01   70bd.9685.9c8f   Better signal

Show & Status.desc: Internal DHCP pool status. Used when WLC serves DHCP for the auth-failed quarantine VLAN.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show ip dhcp pool AUTH-FAILED-POOL
Pool AUTH-FAILED-POOL :
 Utilization mark (high/low)    : 100 / 0
 Subnet size (first/next)       : 0 / 0
 Total addresses                : 253
 Leased addresses               : 5
 Excluded addresses             : 9
 Pending event                  : none
 1 subnet is currently in the pool :
 Current index        IP address range                    Leased/Excluded/Total
 10.6.8.10            10.6.8.1     - 10.6.8.254          5 / 9 / 253

Show & Status.desc: Show only the config block relevant to a keyword. Far faster than scrolling through the whole running config.
Show & Status.example:
WLC-01# show running-config | section radius
WLC-01# show running-config | section wlan
WLC-01# show running-config | include tag

Show & Status.cmd: show ap dot11 24ghz summary (with channel/power asterisk legend)
Show & Status.desc: Per-radio summary. Asterisks (*) on channel or power indicate manually assigned (not RRM-controlled). TX power format: <current_level>/<max_level> (<dBm>); level 1 = max, level 8 = min.
Show & Status.example:
* global assignment
AP Name       Mac Address     Slot  Admin State  Oper State  Width  Txpwr          Channel    Mode
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1     Enabled      Up          40     3/8 (14 dBm)   (149,153)  REAP

AP Management.desc: Rename an AP. Useful after replacing hardware — keeps the policy/site/RF-tag bindings.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-TEMP-01 name AP-OFFICE-01
WLC-01# show ap summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Registered

AP Management.desc: Set the AP location string (max 256 chars). Visible in WLC, Cisco DNAC, and Prime Infrastructure for map placement.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 location "London HQ – 3rd Floor – East Wing"
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Location
  AP Location                            : London HQ – 3rd Floor – East Wing

AP Management.desc: Set primary controller for an AP. AP joins this controller first on reboot.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 controller primary WLC-01 10.10.0.10
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 controller secondary WLC-02 10.10.0.11
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Controller
  Primary Cisco Controller Name        : WLC-01
  Primary Cisco Controller IP Address  : 10.10.0.10
  Secondary Cisco Controller Name      : WLC-02
  Secondary Cisco Controller IP Address: 10.10.0.11

AP Management.desc: Reboot an AP. Tears down the CAPWAP tunnel; clients on this AP will deauth and roam to neighbours.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 reset
WLC-01# show ap summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Joining
# After ~30 seconds:
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Registered

AP Management.desc: Admin-disable an AP. Both radios off; CAPWAP stays up. Use for maintenance.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1   Disabled  Down  40   -  -  REAP

AP Management.desc: Re-enable a previously shutdown AP. Radios come back up in ~5 seconds; clients re-associate.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3/8 (14 dBm)  (149,153)  REAP

AP Management.desc: Pin 2.4 GHz channel manually. Asterisk in `show ap dot11 24ghz summary` indicates manual assignment.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz channel 6
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Enabled  Up  20  3/8 (14 dBm)  6*  REAP
# Revert to RRM control:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz channel global

AP Management.desc: Pin 5 GHz channel manually. Use only when RRM picks a suboptimal channel (e.g. due to recurring DFS hits or known interferer).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz channel 36
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3/8 (14 dBm)  (36,40)*  REAP
# Asterisk (36,40)* = manually pinned; not under RRM/DCA control.
# Revert with: ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz channel global

AP Management.desc: Manual TX power level 1-8 (1 = max, 8 = min). `global` reverts to RRM/RF-profile control.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz txpower 3
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Enabled  Up  20  3*/8 (14 dBm)  6  REAP
# (3*) = manually pinned power; not under RRM/TPC control.

AP Management.desc: Manual 5 GHz TX power level 1-8 (1 = max, 8 = min). `global` reverts to RRM/RF-profile.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz txpower 3
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3*/8 (14 dBm)  (149,153)  REAP
# Revert: ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz txpower global

AP Management.desc: Disable 2.4 GHz radio on a single AP. Useful for testing or band-shaping.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Disabled  Down  -  -  -  REAP
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no dot11 24ghz shutdown   ; re-enable

AP Management.desc: Disable the 5 GHz radio on a single AP. Other radios (2.4 GHz, 6 GHz) remain up.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Disabled  Down  -  -  -  REAP
# Re-enable:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no dot11 5ghz shutdown

AP Management.desc: Set AP to Local mode (central switching). Standard for site-wide deployments where the WLC is on the same campus and clients tunnel back to the WLC.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode local
Changing the AP's mode will cause the AP to reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Local
  Operation State                         : Joining
# After ~30 sec reboot completes:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Local
  Operation State                         : Registered

AP Management.desc: Set AP to FlexConnect mode — local switching at the branch. Required for remote sites with high WAN latency.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode flexconnect
AP will reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
# After reboot, confirm Flex profile is bound via site tag:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                            : FlexConnect

AP Management.desc: Monitor mode — no client service, only RRM/WIPS scanning. Used as a dedicated scanner AP for high-fidelity RF visibility.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode monitor
Changing the AP's mode will cause the AP to reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Monitor
# Monitor APs scan ALL channels but serve NO clients — useful at
# 1:10 ratio with serving APs for full rogue/interferer coverage.

AP Management.desc: Per-AP site-tag override (otherwise inherited from default).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag site SITE-FL1
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  RF-DEFAULT  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Per-AP policy-tag override (otherwise inherited from default-policy-tag).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag policy POLICY-FL1
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  RF-DEFAULT  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Assigns an RF tag to a specific AP
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag rf HIGH-DENSITY
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  HIGH-DENSITY  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Turn off the AP status LED. Useful in conference rooms or where the LED is distracting.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 led disable
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include LED
  AP LED State                        : Disabled

AP Management.desc: AP join priority when the AP load is at limit. 1 = highest, 4 = lowest. Lower-priority APs are kicked off first when the WLC hits its AP cap.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 priority 1
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Priority
  AP Priority                          : 1

AP Management.desc: Single-AP summary view. Faster than scrolling `show ap summary` on a large WLC.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 summary
AP Name           Slots AP Model       Ethernet MAC    Radio MAC     IP Address    State
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AP-OFFICE-01      3     CW9166I-ROW    70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20 10.16.2.11   Registered

AP Management.desc: Same comprehensive AP config view (alias of `show ap config general <ap>`).
