R-neighbour# show ip eigrp topology | include 10.99
(nothing — never advertised)

What it means:
- Filters which prefixes leave this router on the named interface.
- EIGRP packets carrying the filtered routes are simply not sent —
  cheaper than learning then dropping at the receiver.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Manually increases the metric of specific routes to influence path selection without changing interface bandwidth.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config)# access-list 5 permit 10.5.5.0 0.0.0.255
R1(config-router)# offset-list 5 out 1000 GigabitEthernet0/0

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp | include 10.5.5
D    10.5.5.0/24 [90/3816] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← +1000 added

What it means:
- Adds a fixed offset to the metric of routes matching the ACL.
- Use to make this path appear less attractive than another available
  path WITHOUT changing interface bandwidth (which would have side
  effects on QoS, IP SLA, etc.).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Forces the router to advertise itself as the next hop; commonly used in Hub-and-Spoke topologies.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R-Hub(config-if)# ip next-hop-self eigrp 100

R-spoke# show ip route eigrp
D 10.2.2.0/24 [90/3072] via 10.1.1.1, Gi0/0   ← hub IP, not spoke A

What it means:
- ON by default. EIGRP advertises the path's actual next-hop normally,
  which on a multipoint hub-and-spoke means the hub tells spoke B that
  spoke A is the next hop for spoke A's network.
- Spoke B has no L2 reachability to spoke A on DMVPN Phase 1, so the
  hub MUST keep next-hop-self ON.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables syslog messages for neighbor status changes (default in newer IOS).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router)# eigrp log-neighbor-changes

%DUAL-5-NBRCHANGE: EIGRP-IPv4 100: Neighbor 10.1.1.2 (Gi0/0) is up: new adjacency
%DUAL-5-NBRCHANGE: EIGRP-IPv4 100: Neighbor 10.1.1.2 (Gi0/0) is down: holding time expired

What it means:
- Severity 5 syslog message every time an adjacency comes UP or goes
  DOWN, with the trigger reason embedded in the text.
- Enabled by default on modern IOS but always include the line so
  the configuration is explicit.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables EIGRP Named Mode, the modern way to configure EIGRP for both IPv4 and IPv6 in one place.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config)# router eigrp MY_NETWORK
R1(config-router)# address-family ipv4 unicast autonomous-system 100
R1(config-router-af)#

What it means:
- "MY_NETWORK" is a friendly local name for the EIGRP virtual instance.
- AS number moves to the address-family — single config block now
  hosts both IPv4 and IPv6 unicast (and VRFs).
- Replaces the legacy "router eigrp <as>" + "ipv6 router eigrp" pair.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables IPv4 routing within a Named Mode configuration.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router)# address-family ipv4 unicast autonomous-system 100
R1(config-router-af)#

What it means:
- Activates the IPv4 unicast routing context. AS 100 is the value
  carried on the wire; remote peers must match it.
- "topology base" and "af-interface" sub-modes live inside this AF.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables IPv6 routing within a Named Mode configuration, replacing the old ipv6 router eigrp.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router)# address-family ipv6 unicast autonomous-system 100
R1(config-router-af)#

What it means:
- Same as the IPv4 AF but for IPv6 unicast routes.
- Allows IPv4 and IPv6 EIGRP to share router-id, key-chains, and AS
  number under a single "router eigrp" parent.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enters interface-specific config within Named Mode (where bandwidth, hellos, and auth are set).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# af-interface GigabitEthernet0/0
R1(config-router-af-interface)# hello-interval 5
R1(config-router-af-interface)# authentication mode hmac-sha-256 SECRET

What it means:
- Replaces the classic "ip *-eigrp* <as>" interface commands. ALL
  per-interface EIGRP knobs (timers, auth, summary) live here.
- "af-interface default" applies settings to every EIGRP-enabled
  interface unless overridden by a specific af-interface block.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enters topology-specific config within Named Mode for variance, redistributing, and filtering.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# topology base
R1(config-router-af-topology)# variance 2
R1(config-router-af-topology)# redistribute static metric 10000 100 255 1 1500

What it means:
- "Base" is the default topology (analogous to global RIB). Multi-
  Topology Routing (MTR) can host additional topology contexts.
- Variance, redistribution, distribute-list, and offset-list all live
  inside topology mode in Named Mode.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables Wide Metrics in Named Mode, allowing for high-speed interfaces (100Gbps+) without metric saturation.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# metric version 64bit

R1# show ip protocols | include metric
  EIGRP-IPv4 Protocol for AS(100)
    Metric: Maximum hops 100
    Metric: Composite: 64bit

R# show ip route eigrp | include 10.5.5
D 10.5.5.0/24 [90/13107200] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← 64-bit metric

What it means:
- Default 32-bit metrics saturate around 256 Mbps × delay (anything
  above ~256 Mbps gets the same metric).
- 64-bit Wide Metrics keep linear granularity from 1 Gbps up through
  100 Gbps and beyond.
- All routers in the AS should agree; mixed mode works but reduces
  to the lowest version on the path.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables LFA (Loop-Free Alternate) calculation to provide immediate backup paths during a failure.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# fast-reroute per-prefix all

R1# show ip eigrp topology frr
P 10.5.5.0/24, 1 successors, FD is 2816, frr-prot
        via 10.1.1.2 (2816/2570), Gi0/0  ← primary
   FRR LFA: via 10.2.2.2 (3500/2570), Gi0/1  ← backup ready

What it means:
- EIGRP pre-computes a Feasible Successor and pre-programs it as the
  CEF backup next-hop for each protected prefix.
- On primary-link failure, CEF flips traffic in microseconds without
  waiting for DUAL to converge.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Optimizes backup paths to ensure that traffic is balanced effectively during a failover event.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# fast-reroute load-sharing balance

R1# show ip eigrp topology frr | include load
  FRR load-sharing: balance

What it means:
- During the brief LFA failover, multiple LFA paths share the load
  proportionally instead of all traffic flooding the single best LFA.
- Reduces transient congestion when a primary link drops.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Specifies the metric for a summary route in Named Mode, overriding the default lowest-component metric.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# summary-metric 10.0.0.0/8 10000 10 255 1 1500

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp | include 10.0.0.0
D 10.0.0.0/8 [90/2570] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← uses configured metric

What it means:
- Default summary metric is the lowest component metric across the
  contributing prefixes — often hides real cost.
- Setting it explicitly lets you advertise the summary with a
  representative or strategically-chosen metric.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Configures a static neighbor, disabling multicast EIGRP on that interface.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# neighbor 10.1.1.2 GigabitEthernet0/0

R1# show ip eigrp neighbors detail
H  Address    Interface  Hold Uptime    SRTT  RTO  Q  Seq Num
0  10.1.1.2   Gi0/0      12   00:04:15  4     100  0  12
   Static neighbor (Restart time 00:00:00)

What it means:
- Forces unicast Hellos to 10.1.1.2 — switches off the default
  multicast (224.0.0.10).
- Use on NBMA topologies where multicast doesn't traverse the cloud,
  or to restrict who can become a neighbour by IP.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Verifies IPv4 neighbors in Named Mode; standard show ip eigrp commands also work but this is the correct syntax.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 neighbors

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Address-Family Neighbors for AS(100)
H  Address    Interface  Hold Uptime    SRTT  RTO  Q   Seq Num
0  10.2.2.2   Gi0/1      12   00:08:12  4     100  0   12

What it means:
- Same data as "show ip eigrp neighbors" but in Named Mode-aware
  form (shows the VR — Virtual Router — name).
- Required when the same router runs multiple Named Mode VR
  instances simultaneously.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Displays the Named Mode topology table, including the new 64-bit Wide metrics.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 topology

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1)

P 10.5.5.0/24, 1 successors, FD is 13107200, serno 12
        via 10.1.1.2 (13107200/12860160), Gi0/0

What it means:
- Same fields as classic topology output but the FD/RD numbers are
  64-bit Wide Metrics (much larger numerical range).
- Used to confirm Wide Metrics is on and computing values that won't
  saturate on 100 G+ interfaces.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding), allowing the router to keep forwarding traffic during a supervisor failover.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# graceful-restart

R1# show ip eigrp neighbors detail | include restart
   Restart time: 240
   GR support: Yes (helper-only)

What it means:
- Tells neighbours: "if I crash and restart, hold the adjacency
  for up to 240 s while my new RP rebuilds state — don't reset!"
- Both ends should support GR. Pair with a chassis that has dual
  RPs ("redundancy" mode SSO).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Disables a specific address-family or interface within EIGRP without deleting the configuration.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-interface)# shutdown

R1# show ip eigrp interfaces
EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Address-Family Interfaces for AS(100)
                Xmit Queue  PeerQ       Mean   Pacing Time   Multicast
Interface Peers Un/Reliable Un/Reliable SRTT   Un/Reliable   Flow Timer
                              ! shut interface absent

What it means:
- Administratively disables the AF or interface — config is preserved
  for later "no shutdown".
- Use to gracefully de-peer for maintenance without losing the
  configuration.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Configures EIGRP within a VRF, enabling network virtualisation and overlapping IP space.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router)# address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER-A autonomous-system 100
R1(config-router-af)# network 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255

R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER-A neighbors
H  Address    Interface  Hold Uptime    SRTT  RTO  Q  Seq Num
0  10.1.1.2   Gi0/0.10   12   00:04:15  4     100  0  12

What it means:
- Each VRF gets its own EIGRP topology + RIB, isolated from other
  VRFs on the same router.
- Required for MPLS L3VPN PE routers running EIGRP-as-PE-CE.
- AS number can repeat across VRFs because they are isolated.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Scales EIGRP metrics to fit into the 32-bit RIB when using 64-bit Wide Metrics.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# metric rib-scale 128

R1# show ip route 10.5.5.0
Routing entry for 10.5.5.0/24
  Known via "eigrp 100", distance 90, metric 102400 (scaled by 128)
  Tag 0, type internal

What it means:
- The IOS Global RIB stores metrics as 32-bit; raw 64-bit Wide values
  would overflow.
- rib-scale divides the wide metric before installation. Default 128
  preserves enough resolution for typical comparisons.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Configures EIGRP Over-the-Top (OTP), allowing EIGRP neighbors to form over a L3 cloud without direct L2.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 GigabitEthernet0/0 remote 10

R1# show ip eigrp neighbors detail | include OTP
  Remote Static neighbor (max-hops 10) — OTP

What it means:
- "remote 10" allows the EIGRP adjacency to form across up to 10 IP
  hops (not directly attached). The TTL on Hellos is set accordingly.
- Used in EIGRP Over-the-Top (OTP) — runs an EIGRP overlay over a
  generic IP cloud (DMVPN, MPLS, internet) without GRE/IPsec.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Allows specific routes to be advertised from a stub router that would otherwise be suppressed.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config)# route-map ALLOW-LIST permit 10
R1(config-route-map)# match ip address 5
R1(config-router-af)# eigrp stub connected leak-map ALLOW-LIST

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp | include 192.0.2
D 192.0.2.0/24 [90/2816] via 10.1.1.1, Gi0/0   ← leaked

What it means:
- Stub normally only advertises connected/static; leak-map permits
  named non-default routes through anyway.
- Useful when an access-layer stub router has a unique transit-only
  route the rest of the network needs to reach.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Applies commands to all EIGRP interfaces simultaneously within Named Mode.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# af-interface default
R1(config-router-af-interface)# authentication mode hmac-sha-256 SHARED-KEY
R1(config-router-af-interface)# hello-interval 5

What it means:
- Settings applied here become the default for every EIGRP-enabled
  interface in this AF.
- Specific "af-interface <name>" blocks override the default for that
  interface only — fall-through inheritance.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Modifies the Admin Distance of a specific summary route to control route preference.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# summary-metric 10.0.0.0/8 distance 120

R1# show ip route 10.0.0.0
Routing entry for 10.0.0.0/8 — known via "eigrp 100", distance 120

What it means:
- Default AD for EIGRP summary is 5 (intentionally low so it always
  wins on the originator). Bumping it to 120 makes the summary lose
  to other protocols (OSPF, BGP) for the same prefix.
- Use to expose externally-learned summaries while keeping the
  EIGRP-internal more-specifics local.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Sets how long to keep routes from a restarting neighbor during Non-Stop Forwarding.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# timers nsf route-hold 240

R1# show ip protocols | include NSF
  EIGRP NSF-aware route hold timer is 240
  EIGRP NSF helper mode is enabled

What it means:
- During an NSF restart of the peer, this router keeps using the
  routes the peer advertised for up to 240 s.
- If the peer doesn't fully re-establish in time, those routes are
  removed and traffic is reconverged elsewhere.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Adjusts the wait time for a restarting neighbor to signal it has finished its initial update.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# timers nsf signal 20

R1# show ip protocols | include NSF
  EIGRP NSF signal timer is 20

What it means:
- After the restarting peer comes back, this router waits 20 s for
  the END-OF-TABLE signal before declaring NSF a failure.
- Lower for fast tear-down on hangs; raise for very large tables.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables high-strength SHA-256 authentication (Classic Mode syntax) for maximum security.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# ip authentication mode eigrp 1 hmac-sha-256 SECRETKEY2026

R1# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Gi0/0 | include Authentication
  Authentication mode is HMAC-SHA-256

What it means:
- HMAC-SHA-256 is far stronger than legacy MD5 and supported on modern
  IOS XE.
- Inline password syntax (Classic Mode) — no key-chain object needed,
  but you give up rotation flexibility.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enables SHA-256 in Named Mode; significantly more secure than legacy MD5.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-interface)# authentication mode hmac-sha-256 STRONG-SECRET

R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 interfaces detail Gi0/0 | include auth
  Authentication mode is HMAC-SHA-256

What it means:
- Named Mode equivalent of the Classic Mode hmac-sha-256 command.
- Place under "af-interface <X>" or "af-interface default" so all
  matching interfaces inherit it.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Permits the router to accept a default route from neighbors (used when filtering is strict).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# default-information allowed in

R1# show ip route eigrp | include 0.0.0.0
D*   0.0.0.0/0 [90/2816] via 10.1.1.1, Gi0/0   ← accepted

What it means:
- Without this, default-information filtering may strip incoming
  default routes — the router silently ignores any 0.0.0.0/0 EIGRP
  advertises.
- Allow inbound on edge routers that need a default from upstream.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Allows the router to advertise a default route to neighbors.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# default-information allowed out

R-spoke# show ip route eigrp | include 0.0.0.0
D*   0.0.0.0/0 [90/2816] via 10.1.1.1, Gi0/0   ← seen

What it means:
- Permits this router to send 0.0.0.0/0 as an EIGRP route to peers.
- Pair with "ip default-network" or "ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 …" so
  there is actually a default to advertise.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Modifies default AD (90/170) for all EIGRP routes to influence selection against OSPF/BGP.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# distance eigrp 80 160

R1# show ip protocols | include Distance
  Distance: internal 80 external 160

What it means:
- Internal EIGRP routes now have AD 80 (was 90); external EIGRP
  routes have AD 160 (was 170).
- Lowers AD below OSPF (110) so EIGRP is preferred when both protos
  are present in the same RIB. Use carefully — easy way to create
  redistribution loops.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Advertises a manual default route from a specific interface to a neighbor.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# ip summary-address eigrp 100 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0

R-spoke# show ip route eigrp | include 0.0.0.0
D*   0.0.0.0/0 [5/2816] via 10.1.1.1, Gi0/0
                ↑ AD 5 — summary

What it means:
- Generates a default route as a "summary" out this interface only.
- AD is the special "summary AD" of 5 — wins almost everything,
  pinning the spoke to this specific hub link as the default path.

Routing › EIGRP.cmd: show eigrp address-family ipv4 vrf [name] neighbors
Routing › EIGRP.desc: Verifies neighbors within a specific VRF; essential for MPLS/VPN troubleshooting.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER-A neighbors

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Address-Family Neighbors for AS(100)
   VRF(CUSTOMER-A)
H  Address    Interface     Hold  Uptime    SRTT  RTO  Q   Seq Num
0  10.1.1.2   Gi0/0.10      12    00:04:15  4     100  0   12

What it means:
- Filters neighbours to only those within the named VRF — essential
  on PE routers running multiple customer VRFs.
- "VRF(CUSTOMER-A)" header confirms the lookup scope.

Routing › EIGRP.cmd: show eigrp address-family ipv4 topology summary
Routing › EIGRP.desc: Provides a count of routes in the topology table (Active, Passive, etc.) to check stability.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 topology summary

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Topology Table Summary
  Routes:
     Total:     450
     Passive:   450
     Active:    0
     Stub:      0
     Pending:   0

What it means:
- Quick health check — Active count >0 means at least one route is
  in DUAL active state and waiting for replies.
- Active >0 for >30s on a stable network is a flag for SIA risk.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Logs warnings for Hello packet issues at specified intervals.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# eigrp log-neighbor-warnings 60

%DUAL-4-WARNING: EIGRP-IPv4 100: Neighbor 10.1.1.2: K-value mismatch (every 60s)

What it means:
- Repeating warnings (k-value mismatch, auth fail, AS mismatch) get
  rate-limited to one log line per neighbour per 60 s.
- Keeps the syslog usable when a misconfigured peer is constantly
  attempting to come up.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Shows detailed DUAL stats for a single route, including all available sources and RD/FD.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp topology 10.5.5.0/24

EIGRP-IPv4 Topology Entry for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1) for 10.5.5.0/24
  State is Passive, Query origin flag is 1, 1 Successor(s), FD is 3072
  Routing Descriptor Blocks:
  10.1.1.2 (GigabitEthernet0/0), from 10.1.1.2, Send flag is 0x0
      Composite metric is (3072/512), route is Internal
      Vector metric:
        Minimum bandwidth is 1000000 Kbit
        Total delay is 20 microseconds

What it means:
- Drills into one prefix's DUAL state. RD 512 from neighbour, FD 3072
  computed locally.
- "State Passive" = stable. "Query origin flag" tells you which
  neighbour originated the active state when the route was last
  recomputed.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Shows routes currently in Active state, meaning the router is waiting for queries to return.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp topology active

EIGRP-IPv4 Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1)

A 10.5.5.0/24, 0 successors, FD is Inaccessible
   1 replies, active 00:01:02, query-origin: Local origin
        via 10.1.1.2 (Infinity/Infinity), Gi0/0, serno 22

What it means:
- A = Active state — DUAL has sent Queries and is waiting for Replies
  (or for the SIA timer to expire).
- "active 00:01:02" is concerning — past 3 min the route will go SIA
  and the offending neighbour will be reset.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Finds routes that have no path to the destination but remain in the topology table.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp topology zero-successors

EIGRP-IPv4 Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1)

P 10.5.5.0/24, 0 successors, FD is Inaccessible
        via 10.1.1.2 (Infinity/Infinity), Gi0/0

What it means:
- Routes with FD = Inaccessible — destination is unreachable but
  EIGRP remembers it for SIA-protection / poison-reverse purposes.
- A growing zero-successor list points at chronic upstream churn.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Logs specific neighbor state machine transitions in Named Mode.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# debug eigrp address-family ipv4 neighbor 10.1.1.2

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) AS(100): Nbr 10.1.1.2 retransmit queue empty
EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) AS(100): Nbr 10.1.1.2 going DOWN: holding time expired
EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) AS(100): Nbr 10.1.1.2 going UP

What it means:
- Filtered to a single neighbour — much less noise than a global
  "debug eigrp packets".
- Shows state-machine transitions with the trigger reason.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Shows exactly what the router is sending to its neighbors to find missing advertisements.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# debug eigrp transmit

EIGRP: Send UPDATE on Gi0/1 nbr 10.2.2.2, AS 100 - prefix 10.5.5.0/24 metric 3072
EIGRP: Send QUERY on Gi0/1 nbr 10.2.2.2, AS 100 - prefix 10.6.6.0/24 metric Infinity

What it means:
- Logs every outbound packet by content. Use to confirm whether a
  prefix really is being advertised to a peer.
- Combine with "debug eigrp transmit detail" for full TLV decoding
  (CPU-heavy).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Logs how EIGRP is installing/removing routes from the Global RIB.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# debug eigrp route

EIGRP-IPv4 100: Route 192.168.1.0/24 install: best path via 10.1.1.2
EIGRP-IPv4 100: Route 192.168.1.0/24 redist: External, type 90, metric 3072

What it means:
- Logs the install/remove/update path between the EIGRP topology
  table and the global RIB.
- Use when a route is in the EIGRP topology as Passive but mysteriously
  not in the routing table — log will show why install was rejected.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Displays the internal hello/hold timers and how much time remains for each neighbor.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp timers

EIGRP-IPv4 Timers for AS(100)
  Hello timer: every 5 sec
  Hold timer:  every 15 sec, expires for nbr 10.1.1.2 in 12 sec
  Active timer: 3 min

What it means:
- Snapshot of all process and per-neighbour timers.
- Use when debugging adjacency flaps to see exactly how much hold
  budget is left right now.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Displays the number of routes learned from each neighbor; helps identify heavy peers.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp accounting

EIGRP-IPv4 Accounting for AS(100)
Neighbor       Routes Sent   Updates Sent   Updates Received
10.1.1.1       45            12             3
10.2.2.1       7             4              50   ← heavy upstream peer

What it means:
- Per-neighbour accounting of routes and update counters.
- Quickly spot which peer is providing the bulk of your routes —
  helps capacity-plan summarisation efforts.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Prerequisite command on an interface before EIGRP can form a VRF-lite adjacency.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# ip vrf forwarding RED
R1(config-if)# ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0

R1# show ip route vrf RED
Routing Table: RED
D    10.5.5.0/24 [90/2816] via 10.1.1.2, Gi0/0.10

What it means:
- Binds the interface to VRF "RED" — the EIGRP adjacency formed on
  this interface will be in the VRF's RIB, not the global RIB.
- Must come before "ip address" or the IP gets removed and re-added.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Simulation command to test how a router-id change would affect the process.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# test eigrp address-family ipv4 100 router-id 9.9.9.9

EIGRP test: Router-ID 9.9.9.9 is unique within the AS
            Conflict with existing router-id 1.1.1.1: NO

What it means:
- Dry-run a router-id change without actually applying it.
- Confirms uniqueness across the AS before committing the change —
  saves you from triggering an external-route purge by accident.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Dumps massive amounts of data for Cisco TAC engineers; used for deep crash analysis.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp tech-support

------------------ show eigrp address-family ipv4 timers ------------------
... (extensive multi-page output) ...
------------------ show eigrp address-family ipv4 topology  ----------------
... (etc) ...

What it means:
- Combined dump of every EIGRP show command — packet counters, FSM
  state, internal queues.
- Pipe to a file ("term len 0" first) and attach to the TAC case
  when reporting EIGRP bugs.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Monitors stub router behavior, showing if queries are being correctly suppressed.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# debug eigrp stub

EIGRP-IPv4 100: Stub neighbor 10.1.1.2 — query for 10.5.5.0/24 SUPPRESSED
EIGRP-IPv4 100: Stub neighbor 10.1.1.2 — query for 10.6.6.0/24 SUPPRESSED

What it means:
- Confirms this router is correctly NOT sending Queries toward stub
  neighbours (which is the whole point of stubbing — eliminates
  Queries from reaching the access layer and thus prevents SIA).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Allows EIGRP to consume more than 100% of perceived bandwidth (common on sub-rate circuits).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# bandwidth 56
R1(config-if)# ip bandwidth-percent eigrp 100 200

R1# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Se0/0 | include bandwidth
  Bandwidth percent is 200 (over-subscription allowed)

What it means:
- On a sub-rate WAN circuit (configured "bandwidth 56" while real
  capacity is 1.5 Mbps), EIGRP would self-throttle below the actual
  link speed.
- Setting >100% lets EIGRP use the real bandwidth, not the kbps value.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Verifies Fast Reroute (LFA) backup paths that are pre-programmed for instant failover.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp topology frr

P 10.5.5.0/24, 1 successors, FD 2816, frr-prot
   Successor: via 10.1.1.2 Gi0/0
   FRR LFA  : via 10.2.2.2 Gi0/1   ← pre-installed backup
   Repair Path Type: Per-prefix LFA

What it means:
- Confirms an LFA backup is pre-programmed for the prefix.
- "Repair Path Type" can be Per-prefix, Per-link, or TI-LFA depending
  on the FRR configuration. Per-prefix gives finest granularity.

Routing › EIGRP.cmd: show eigrp address-family ipv4 vrf [name] traffic
Routing › EIGRP.desc: VRF-specific packet counters to ensure hello/update packets are not being dropped by an ACL.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER-A traffic

EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) AS(100) Traffic Statistics  VRF(CUSTOMER-A)
  Hellos sent/received: 312/312
  Updates sent/received: 10/10
  Queries sent/received: 0/0

What it means:
- Per-VRF packet counters. If sent ≫ received, an ACL or firewall
  on the path is probably dropping inbound EIGRP packets.
- 0 Queries on a stable network is the expected steady state.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Adjusts the global pool of bandwidth EIGRP can use for control plane traffic.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# max-reserved-bandwidth 75

R1# show interface Gi0/0 | include reserved
  Reserved bandwidth: 75 percent

What it means:
- Maximum proportion of interface bandwidth reservable for QoS class
  queues.
- Default is 75%. Bump up only when you genuinely need most of the
  link for prioritised classes (VoIP, video).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Forces the router to set its own IP as the next hop in a hub-and-spoke DMVPN.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R-Hub(config-router-af-interface)# no next-hop-self

R-spoke# show ip route eigrp | include 10.5.5
D 10.5.5.0/24 [90/3072] via 10.6.6.6, Gi0/0   ← original spoke A IP

What it means:
- DEFAULT is next-hop-self ON. Turning it OFF tells the hub to keep
  the original next-hop (spoke A) when re-advertising spoke A's
  routes to spoke B.
- DMVPN Phase 2/3: spokes can NHRP-resolve each other and build
  direct tunnels — turn OFF so spoke B takes the optimal path
  directly to spoke A.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Disables Split Horizon per neighbor (Named Mode); vital for DMVPN Phase 2.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R-Hub(config-router-af-interface)# no split-horizon

R-Hub# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Tu0 | include Split
  Split-horizon is disabled

What it means:
- Hub MUST disable split-horizon on multipoint Tu0 so it can
  re-advertise spoke A's routes back out the same interface to
  spoke B.
- Without this, spokes can never see each other's routes regardless
  of NHRP / next-hop-self settings.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Redistributes static routes with a specific seed metric.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# redistribute static metric 1000 1 255 1 1500

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp | include EX
D EX 192.0.2.0/24 [170/2816] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0

What it means:
- Imports static routes into EIGRP with the specified seed metric.
- Without an explicit metric (and no default-metric), static routes
  are silently dropped — common gotcha.
- Result is an external EIGRP route (AD 170, "EX" code).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Quickly injects local subnets into the EIGRP domain.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# redistribute connected

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp | include EX
D EX 192.168.10.0/24 [170/2816] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0

What it means:
- Pushes every directly-connected subnet (not already covered by a
  network statement) into EIGRP as an external route.
- Quick and dirty alternative to listing every loopback / interface
  subnet under "network" statements.
- Filter aggressively with route-maps to avoid leaking management
  IPs.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Named Mode version of summarization with granular route leaking control.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# summary-address 172.16.0.0/16 leak-map ALLOW-MGMT

R-neighbour# show ip route eigrp
D    172.16.0.0/16 [90/2816] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0     ← summary
D    172.16.99.0/24 [90/3072] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← leaked specific

What it means:
- Sends a single 172.16.0.0/16 plus the explicitly leaked specifics.
- Use when you want summary efficiency on most prefixes but keep one
  or two more-specifics for backup-path or monitoring purposes.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Gives certain prefixes priority during SPF/DUAL calculation for faster convergence on critical routes.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config)# access-list 50 permit host 10.10.10.10
R1(config-router-af)# prefix-priority high 50

R1# show ip eigrp prefix-priority
   High-priority prefixes: 1
   List 50: 10.10.10.10/32

What it means:
- DUAL processes high-priority prefixes first during a topology
  change — Voice, Video, control-plane routes converge before
  bulk data.
- Use when sub-second voice/video convergence matters and you can
  rank a small subset of prefixes as "critical".

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Limits the number of concurrent DUAL active processes to protect router CPU.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# active-process-limit 10

R1# show ip protocols | include active
   EIGRP active-process-limit is 10

What it means:
- During mass topology change, DUAL would normally try to recompute
  every affected prefix simultaneously and spike CPU.
- Capping concurrency at 10 forces the recompute to serialise —
  smoother CPU, slightly slower convergence on the long-tail.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Adds a delay between EIGRP packets to prevent overwhelming a neighbor's CPU.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# timers throttle packets 50

R1# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Gi0/0 | include Pacing
  Pacing time : 50ms (Un/Reliable)

What it means:
- Inserts 50 ms gaps between successive EIGRP packets on the wire.
- Useful when peering with low-end CPE devices that drop packets
  arriving back-to-back.

Routing › EIGRP.cmd: show ip eigrp topology summary vrf [name]
Routing › EIGRP.desc: Checks VRF health by looking at the total number of entries in that specific table.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1# show ip eigrp topology summary vrf CUSTOMER-A

EIGRP-IPv4 Topology Table Summary for VRF CUSTOMER-A
   Total prefixes: 124
   Active prefixes: 0
   Stuck-in-Active: 0

What it means:
- Per-VRF roll-up. 0 Active and 0 SIA on a quiet domain is what you
  want.
- "Total prefixes" tracks growth — sudden jumps can flag
  redistribution loops.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Interface command to apply a rotation of keys for security compliance.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-if)# ip authentication key-chain eigrp 100 EIGRP-KEYS

R1# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Gi0/0 | include key-chain
  Authentication key-chain is "EIGRP-KEYS"

What it means:
- Binds the named key-chain object to EIGRP on this interface.
- The key-chain object itself is created with "key chain X / key 1 /
  key-string …" plus accept- / send-lifetime windows for graceful
  rotation.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Extends EIGRP's reach beyond the default 100 hops (useful for massive global networks).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# metric maximum-hops 200

R1# show ip protocols | include hops
  Maximum hops: 200

What it means:
- EIGRP records hop count in its metric. Routes beyond the maximum
  are declared unreachable (Infinity).
- Default is 100 — fine for most networks. Bump up for very large
  layered topologies (some MPLS underlays).

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Changes AD for specific neighbors only; used to prefer one site over another.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af-topology)# distance 85 10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0

R1# show ip route 10.5.5.0
Routing entry for 10.5.5.0/24
  Known via "eigrp 100", distance 85, metric 2816   ← AD lowered

What it means:
- Routes received from neighbour 10.1.1.1 (specifically — wildcard
  0.0.0.0) get AD 85 instead of the default 90.
- Lets you prefer one upstream site over another without changing
  metric or prefix lists.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Named Mode command for setting the ID inside the Address-Family.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router-af)# eigrp router-id 2.2.2.2

R1# show eigrp address-family ipv4 topology | include ID
EIGRP-IPv4 VR(MY_NETWORK) Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(2.2.2.2)

What it means:
- Sets the router-id inside the Named Mode AF (different scope from
  Classic Mode "eigrp router-id" under "router eigrp").
- The router-id stamps external routes — must be unique per VR/AF.

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Hardens the router by disabling EIGRP on all ports, requiring manual no passive on intended ports.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
R1(config-router)# passive-interface default
R1(config-router)# no passive-interface GigabitEthernet0/0

R1# show ip protocols | include passive
   Passive Interface(s):
     GigabitEthernet0/1
     GigabitEthernet0/2
     ...
   Active interfaces: GigabitEthernet0/0

What it means:
- Inverts the default — every interface is passive UNLESS explicitly
  un-passived.
- Prevents accidental adjacencies on unintended interfaces (rogue
  router on a spare port, etc.). Best-practice on access-layer routers.

Routing › BGP.desc: Full BGP table (IPv4 unicast, default VRF). Each prefix shows all known paths with `>` marking best.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1
Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, i - internal,
              r RIB-failure, S Stale, m multipath, b backup-path, f RT-Filter,
              x best-external, a additional-path, c RIB-compressed,
Origin codes: i - IGP, e - EGP, ? - incomplete

     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>e 8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 15169 i
 *>e 192.0.2.0/24     10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 i
 *e  192.0.2.0/24     10.0.0.6                 0             0 65003 65002 i
 *>i 10.50.0.0/16     1.1.1.1                  0    100      0 i

Routing › BGP.desc: BGP peer summary — V/AS/MsgRcvd/MsgSent/TblVer/InQ/OutQ/Up-Down/State or PfxRcd.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp summary
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
BGP table version is 12345
234 network entries using 56160 bytes of memory

Neighbor    V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.5    4 65002    98421    98412    12345    0    0  3w2d           512
10.0.0.6    4 65003     1234     1230    12345    0    0  00:00:14    Idle

Routing › BGP.desc: Full detail for every BGP peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp neighbors | head -20
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.5, remote AS 65002, external link
  BGP version 4, remote router ID 1.2.3.5
  BGP state = Established, up for 3w2d
  Last read 00:00:23, last write 00:00:18
  Hold time is 180, keepalive interval is 60 seconds
  Neighbor capabilities:
    Route refresh: advertised and received(new)
    Four-octets ASN Capability: advertised and received
    Address family IPv4 Unicast: advertised and received

Routing › BGP.desc: Shows neighbor details
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.5, remote AS 65002, external link
  BGP state = Established, up for 3w2d
  Last reset by us never, due to No error
  Connection state is ESTAB, I/O status: 1, unread input bytes: 0
  TCP MD5 password not in use

Routing › BGP.desc: Current BGP dampening parameters (half-life, suppress, reuse, max-suppress).
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp dampening parameters
 dampening 15 750 2000 60 (route-map none)
  Half-life time      :    15 mins       Decay Time       : 2320 secs
  Max suppress penalty: 12000            Max suppress time:   60 mins
  Suppress penalty :   2000              Reuse penalty :      750

Routing › BGP.desc: Display all AS_PATH values currently in the BGP path-attribute database with reference counts. Useful for capacity/sizing investigation.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp paths
Address     Refcount   Metric   Path
0x12345abc  1234       0        65002 65003 i
0x23456bcd  876        0        65002 15169 i
0x34567cde  543        0        65001 i

Routing › BGP.desc: BGP table filtered by community.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp community 65001:200
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1

     Network          Next Hop       Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>i 10.50.0.0/16     1.1.1.2             0    100      0 i
      Community: 65001:200

Routing › BGP.desc: Filter BGP by AS-path regex.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp regexp _65002_
     Network          Next Hop       Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>e 8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5             0           0 65002 15169 i

Routing › BGP.desc: BGP table within a VRF.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp vrf TENANT-A
BGP routing table information for VRF TENANT-A
     Network          Next Hop       Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>  10.50.0.0/24     0.0.0.0              0         32768 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Full VPNv4 BGP table across all VRFs.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show ip bgp vpnv4 all
BGP table version is 5432, local router ID is 1.1.1.1
Route Distinguisher: 65001:1 (default for vrf TENANT-A)
 *> 10.50.0.0/24     0.0.0.0              0         32768 i
 *>i 10.50.10.0/24   2.2.2.2              0    100      0 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Newer AF-style summary (mirror of `show ip bgp summary` but uses AF syntax).
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
Neighbor    V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.5    4 65002    98421    98412    12345    0    0  3w2d           512

Routing › BGP.desc: Best/alt paths and attributes for a prefix
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show bgp ipv4 unicast 8.8.8.0/24
BGP routing table entry for 8.8.8.0/24, version 9876
Paths: (1 available, best #1)
  AS-Path: 65002 15169, path sourced external
  10.0.0.5 from 10.0.0.5 (1.2.3.5)
      Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 100, weight 0, valid, external, best

Routing › BGP.desc: Soft-reset a BGP neighbor without bouncing session
Routing › BGP.example: sw# clear bgp ipv4 unicast 10.0.0.5 soft

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter BGP process for local AS. Only one BGP process per router on IOS-XE.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# router bgp 65001
router(config-router)# bgp router-id 1.1.1.1
router(config-router)# bgp log-neighbor-changes
router(config-router)# no synchronization
router(config-router)# no auto-summary
router# show ip bgp summary | include router|AS
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001

Routing › BGP.desc: Pin the BGP router-ID. Otherwise IOS-XE auto-derives from highest loopback IP, then highest physical IP. Critical for predictable behaviour across reloads.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# bgp router-id 1.1.1.1
router# clear ip bgp *  ; required to take effect
router# show ip bgp summary | include router
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001

Routing › BGP.desc: Log every BGP peer state transition. Cheap, always-on visibility.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# bgp log-neighbor-changes
router# show logging | include BGP
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.5 Up
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.6 Down - User reset
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.6 Up

Routing › BGP.desc: Disable IGP synchronisation — BGP will advertise routes without requiring matching IGP entries. Default in IOS-XE 15+; only relevant on legacy designs.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# no synchronization

Routing › BGP.desc: Define a BGP peer. remote-AS != local-AS = eBGP; equal = iBGP. The fundamental peering statement.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 remote-as 65002
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 description ISP-A primary
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 update-source Loopback0
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include state|AS
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.5,  remote AS 65002,  external link
  BGP state = Established, up for 3w2d

Routing › BGP.desc: Free-text description on a BGP peer. Pure documentation but visible in `show ip bgp neighbors`. Always describe peers.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 description ISP-A primary - 1 Gbps DIA

Routing › BGP.desc: Originate a prefix into BGP. IOS-XE syntax uses `mask <subnet-mask>` (Nexus uses prefix/length). Prefix must exist in the RIB to be advertised.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# network 10.0.0.0 mask 255.255.255.0
router# show ip bgp 10.0.0.0/24
BGP routing table entry for 10.0.0.0/24, version 12345
Paths: (1 available, best #1, table default)
  Advertised to update-groups:  1
  Refresh Epoch 1
  Local
    0.0.0.0 from 0.0.0.0 (1.1.1.1)
      Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 100, weight 32768, valid, sourced, local, best

Routing › BGP.desc: Per-peer detail: capabilities, timers, counters.
Routing › BGP.example:
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.2,  remote AS 65002, external link
  BGP version 4, remote router ID 192.168.5.5
  BGP state = Established, up for 05:22:11
  ...

Routing › BGP.desc: Show prefixes this router has advertised TO a specific peer (after outbound policy). Use to confirm "did my neighbour receive what I think I sent?".
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 advertised-routes
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1
Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best

     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>  10.0.0.0/24      0.0.0.0                  0         32768 i
 *>  10.1.0.0/16      0.0.0.0                  0         32768 i

Total number of prefixes 2

Routing › BGP.desc: Prefixes received from a peer BEFORE inbound policy. Requires `neighbor X.X.X.X soft-reconfiguration inbound` to be enabled (memory cost).
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 received-routes
% Inbound soft reconfiguration not enabled on 10.0.0.5
# Enable first:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 soft-reconfiguration inbound
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 received-routes | head
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
*    8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 15169 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Prefixes received from a peer AFTER inbound policy filtering — the routes actually installed for consideration by best-path.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 routes
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1

     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>e 8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 15169 i
 *>e 192.0.2.0/24     10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 i

Total number of prefixes 2

Routing › BGP.desc: Per-prefix path detail (best-path selection reasoning).
Routing › BGP.example:
BGP routing table entry for 10.1.1.0/24, version 5
Paths: (1 available, best #1)
  65002
    10.0.0.2 from 10.0.0.2 (192.168.1.1)
      Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 100, valid, external, best

Routing › BGP.desc: Filter BGP table by AS-path regex. Use to find which prefixes transit a specific AS.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp regexp _65000_
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>e 8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5                 0             0 65002 65000 15169 i
# Common regexes:
#   ^65002_      = prefixes originated by directly-connected AS 65002
#   _65000$      = prefixes whose path ends in AS 65000
#   _65000_      = prefixes that transit AS 65000
#   ^$           = locally originated prefixes (empty AS_PATH)

Routing › BGP.desc: Hard-reset a BGP peer (tears down and rebuilds the TCP session). Disruptive — drops all routes from that peer until session re-establishes.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# clear ip bgp 10.0.0.5
router# show ip bgp summary | include 10.0.0.5
10.0.0.5        4       65002       0       0        1    0    0 00:00:05 Idle

Routing › BGP.desc: Soft-reset all peers — re-process inbound and outbound policy without dropping sessions. Requires Route-Refresh capability (default since 12.x).
Routing › BGP.example:
router# clear ip bgp * soft
router# show ip bgp summary | include refresh
Neighbor V  AS  MsgRcvd MsgSent  TblVer InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.5  4 65002   98421   98412  12345   0    0 3w2d         4234

Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound soft-refresh on a single peer — re-evaluate the inbound route-map / prefix-list without dropping the session.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# clear ip bgp 10.0.0.5 soft in
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include Refresh
  Route refresh request: received 0, sent 1

Routing › BGP.desc: Outbound soft-refresh — resend the local RIB to a peer after editing outbound policy.
Routing › BGP.example: sw# clear ip bgp 10.0.0.5 soft out

Routing › BGP.desc: Source BGP TCP session from a loopback. Mandatory for iBGP (loopbacks survive link failures). Pair with `ebgp-multihop` if the eBGP peer is not directly attached.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 update-source Loopback0
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include update
  Update source is Loopback0

Routing › BGP.desc: Allow eBGP TCP session to traverse N IP hops (default 1). Required when peering loopback-to-loopback.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 ebgp-multihop 5

Routing › BGP.desc: Rewrite NEXT_HOP on advertise to be this router. ESSENTIAL for iBGP — otherwise iBGP peers receive prefixes with eBGP next-hops they cannot reach.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 next-hop-self
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 advertised-routes | head
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
*>i  8.8.8.0/24       1.1.1.1                  0    100      0 65002 15169 i  ; was 192.0.2.5

Routing › BGP.desc: MD5 authentication on the BGP TCP session. Both peers must use the same password. Mostly historical — TCP-AO is preferred where supported.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 password CISCO123
# Without matching passwords:
%TCP-6-BADAUTH: No MD5 digest from 10.0.0.5(179) to 1.1.1.1(45123)

Routing › BGP.desc: GTSM (Generalised TTL Security Mechanism) — require incoming TTL ≥ 255-hops. Blocks spoofed BGP packets from beyond N hops. Set hops=1 for a directly-attached peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 ttl-security hops 1
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include TTL
  External BGP neighbor may be up to 1 hop away. Send TTL on outgoing packet 255

Routing › BGP.desc: Send standard AND extended communities to this peer. Default is no communities. `both` covers std + ext; `standard` / `extended` for one or the other.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 send-community both
router# show ip bgp 10.0.0.0/24 | include Community
  Community: 65001:100 65001:200 no-export

Routing › BGP.desc: Strip private ASNs (64512-65534, 4200000000-4294967294) from AS_PATH before advertising to this peer. Common on transit/customer edges.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 remove-private-as
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include private
  Removal of private AS number is enabled

Routing › BGP.desc: Cap prefixes received from a peer at 500k; warn at 90% (450k); on cap-hit, tear down and restart in 30 min. Protects RIB/FIB from a misconfigured peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 maximum-prefix 500000 90 restart 30
%BGP-4-MAXPFX: No. of prefix received from 10.0.0.5 (afi 0) reaches 450000, max 500000
%BGP-3-MAXPFXEXCEED: No. of prefix received from 10.0.0.5: 500001 exceed limit 500000
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.5 Down - BGP Notification sent
# Will auto-reconnect after 30 minutes.

Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound route-map filter — modify or drop received prefixes. Common use: scrub communities, restrict accepted prefix list, set local-preference.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map FROM-ISP permit 10
router(config-route-map)#   match ip address prefix-list ACCEPTABLE
router(config-route-map)#   set local-preference 200
router(config-route-map)#   set community 65001:200
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-map FROM-ISP in

Routing › BGP.desc: Outbound route-map filter — modify or restrict what is advertised TO this peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map TO-ISP permit 10
router(config-route-map)#   match ip address prefix-list MY-NETS
router(config-route-map)#   set as-path prepend 65001 65001
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-map TO-ISP out

Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound prefix-list filter — accept only matching prefixes (no modification). Lighter than a route-map when you only need filtering.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw(config)# ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
sw(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 prefix-list PEER-IN in

Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound AS-path filter — accept/deny based on AS_PATH regex. Use to block transit through specific ASNs.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# ip as-path access-list 100 deny _65999_
router(config)# ip as-path access-list 100 permit .*
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 filter-list 100 in

Routing › BGP.cmd: ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
Routing › BGP.desc: Prefix-list entry — accept default through /24. Common shape for an inbound transit filter.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
router(config)# ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 20 deny 0.0.0.0/0 le 32
router# show ip prefix-list PEER-IN
ip prefix-list PEER-IN: 2 entries
   seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
   seq 20 deny 0.0.0.0/0 le 32

Routing › BGP.desc: AS-path ACL — match paths whose first AS is 65002 (directly-connected eBGP origin).
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# ip as-path access-list 100 permit ^65002_
router# show ip as-path-access-list 100
AS path access list 100
    permit ^65002_

Routing › BGP.cmd:
route-map TO-ISP permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list MY-NETS
 set as-path prepend 65001 65001 65001
Routing › BGP.desc: Outbound prepend — make a path less preferred by lengthening its AS_PATH. Triple-prepend is a common pattern for backup links.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map TO-ISP permit 10
router(config-route-map)#  match ip address prefix-list MY-NETS
router(config-route-map)#  set as-path prepend 65001 65001 65001
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-map TO-ISP out
router# show route-map TO-ISP
route-map TO-ISP, permit, sequence 10
  Match clauses:
    ip address prefix-lists: MY-NETS
  Set clauses:
    as-path prepend 65001 65001 65001

Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound LOCAL_PREF — raise to make this path preferred (default LP=100). Higher LP wins in best-path selection.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map FROM-ISP permit 10
router(config-route-map)#  set local-preference 200
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-map FROM-ISP in
router# show ip bgp | include LocPrf
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>e 8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5                 0    200      0 65002 15169 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Tag outbound advertisements with BGP communities — a downstream-controlled signalling mechanism (e.g. "65001:100 = no-export to peers").
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map TO-CUSTOMER permit 10
router(config-route-map)#  set community 65001:100 65001:200
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-map TO-CUSTOMER out
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 send-community both

Routing › BGP.desc: Aggregate /8 from more-specific prefixes and SUPPRESS the contributing routes (summary-only). Without `summary-only` both summary and specifics are advertised.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# aggregate-address 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 summary-only
router# show ip bgp
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>  10.0.0.0/8       0.0.0.0                            32768 i
 s>  10.0.0.0/24      0.0.0.0                            32768 i   ; suppressed
 s>  10.1.0.0/16      0.0.0.0                            32768 i   ; suppressed

Routing › BGP.desc: Aggregate with `as-set` — preserves the set of contributing AS_PATHs (so AS_PATH loop-prevention still works). Use when redistributing eBGP-learned prefixes back into the same fabric.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# aggregate-address 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 as-set
router# show ip bgp 10.0.0.0/8
BGP routing table entry for 10.0.0.0/8, version 12345
Paths: (1 available, best #1, table default, Advertisements suppressed by an aggregate.)
  Advertised to update-groups:  1
  Refresh Epoch 1
  {65002,65003}, (aggregated by 65001 1.1.1.1)
    0.0.0.0 from 0.0.0.0 (1.1.1.1)

Routing › BGP.desc: Redistribute connected into BGP, filtered by route-map. WARNING: redistributing into BGP leaks every connected /24 — always filter.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# route-map CONN-INTO-BGP permit 10
router(config-route-map)#  match interface Loopback0
router(config-router)# redistribute connected route-map CONN-INTO-BGP

Routing › BGP.desc: Mark an iBGP peer as a route-reflector client. The local router reflects routes from this peer to all other clients, removing the iBGP full-mesh requirement.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 route-reflector-client
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.6 route-reflector-client
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include reflector
  Route-Reflector Client

Routing › BGP.desc: Cluster-ID for redundant route-reflectors. Two RRs in the same cluster share a cluster-ID so they suppress duplicate reflections.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# bgp cluster-id 1.1.1.1

Routing › BGP.cmd:
neighbor RR-CLIENTS peer-group
neighbor RR-CLIENTS remote-as 65001
neighbor RR-CLIENTS update-source Loopback0
neighbor RR-CLIENTS route-reflector-client
neighbor 2.2.2.2 peer-group RR-CLIENTS
Routing › BGP.desc: Peer-group for many similar RR clients. Configure once, attach many peers — both more readable and more efficient (BGP update generation is shared per peer-group).
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor RR-CLIENTS peer-group
router(config-router)# neighbor RR-CLIENTS remote-as 65001
router(config-router)# neighbor RR-CLIENTS update-source Loopback0
router(config-router)# neighbor RR-CLIENTS route-reflector-client
router(config-router)# neighbor RR-CLIENTS send-community both
router(config-router)# neighbor 2.2.2.2 peer-group RR-CLIENTS
router(config-router)# neighbor 3.3.3.3 peer-group RR-CLIENTS
router(config-router)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 peer-group RR-CLIENTS

Routing › BGP.desc: eBGP ECMP — install up to 8 equal-cost paths into the RIB.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# maximum-paths 8
router# show ip bgp 8.8.8.0/24 | include path|installed
Paths: (4 available, best #1, table default, multipath)

Routing › BGP.desc: iBGP ECMP — same as `maximum-paths` but for iBGP routes (different best-path rules).
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router-af)# maximum-paths ibgp 8

Routing › BGP.desc: Allow multipath even when AS-paths differ but length matches. Essential in VXLAN-EVPN and any fabric where leafs receive same prefix from spines with different AS_PATHs.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# bgp bestpath as-path multipath-relax

Routing › BGP.desc: BGP graceful-restart — peers preserve forwarding state across a control-plane restart of this router.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# bgp graceful-restart
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include restart
  Graceful Restart Capability: advertised and received
    Remote Restart timer is 120 seconds
    Address families advertised by peer:
      IPv4 Unicast (was preserved)

Routing › BGP.desc: Penalise flapping prefixes — suppress unstable routes from being installed. Out of fashion (today: tighten convergence instead, do not dampen). Mention for legacy networks.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# bgp dampening 15 750 2000 60
router# show ip bgp dampening parameters
  dampening 15 750 2000 60 (route-map none)
    Half-life time      :    15 mins       Decay Time       : 2320 secs
    Max suppress penalty: 12000            Max suppress time:   60 mins
    Suppress penalty :   2000              Reuse penalty :      750

Routing › BGP.desc: Bind BFD to a BGP peer — sub-second failure detection. Pair with `bfd interval ... min_rx ... multiplier ...` on the interface.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 fall-over bfd
router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
router(config-if)# bfd interval 250 min_rx 250 multiplier 3
router# show bfd neighbors application BGP
NeighAddr    LD/RD     RH/RS  State    Int       Vrf
10.0.0.5     4097/4097 Up     Up       Gi0/0/0   default

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter the IPv6 unicast AF inside BGP — required to advertise IPv6 prefixes and activate IPv6 peers.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# address-family ipv6 unicast
router(config-router-af)#   network 2001:db8::/48
  neighbor 2001:db8::5 activate

Routing › BGP.cmd:
neighbor 2001:db8::1 remote-as 65002
address-family ipv6 unicast
 neighbor 2001:db8::1 activate
Routing › BGP.desc: IPv6 BGP peer — define remote-as at process level, activate inside ipv6 unicast AF. Same pattern as MPLS-VPN: peer at process level, activate per-AF.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 2001:db8::5 remote-as 65002
router(config-router)# address-family ipv6 unicast
router(config-router-af)#   neighbor 2001:db8::5 activate
router# show bgp ipv6 unicast summary
Neighbor       V   AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent  TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
2001:DB8::5    4 65002   12345    12340   1234    0    0  3w2d           512

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv6 BGP peer summary.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show bgp ipv6 unicast summary
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
Neighbor       V   AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent  TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
2001:DB8::5    4 65002   12345    12340    1234    0    0  3w2d            512

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv6 BGP table.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw# show bgp ipv6 unicast
     Network          Next Hop       Metric LocPrf Weight Path
*>e  2001:4860::/32   2001:DB8::5         0           0 65002 15169 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter VPNv4 unicast AF — MPLS L3VPN. Peer this AF with PE routers to exchange VPN labels and prefixes.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# address-family vpnv4
router(config-router-af)#   neighbor 1.1.1.2 activate
  neighbor 1.1.1.2 send-community extended
  exit-address-family

Routing › BGP.cmd: show bgp vpnv4 unicast all summary
Routing › BGP.desc: VPNv4 peer summary across all VRFs/RDs.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show bgp vpnv4 unicast all summary
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
BGP table version is 5432, main routing table version 5432

Neighbor    V   AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent  TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
1.1.1.2     4 65001   12345    12340   5432    0    0  3w2d           1234
1.1.1.3     4 65001   12340    12345   5432    0    0  3w2d           1234

Routing › BGP.cmd:
address-family ipv4 vrf TENANT-A
 redistribute connected
 redistribute static
Routing › BGP.desc: Per-VRF BGP AF for MPLS L3VPN PE — redistribute the tenant's connected/static into BGP, which carries them as VPNv4 to other PEs.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# address-family ipv4 vrf TENANT-A
router(config-router-af)#   redistribute connected
  redistribute static
  neighbor 10.50.0.2 remote-as 65100
  neighbor 10.50.0.2 activate
  exit-address-family

Routing › BGP.desc: Administratively shut down a BGP peer — keep config but tear down session. Use during maintenance or to quickly drop a misbehaving peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 shutdown
router# show ip bgp summary | include 10.0.0.5
10.0.0.5  4 65002       0       0        1    0    0 00:00:14 Idle (Admin)

Routing › BGP.desc: Show routes currently being suppressed by route-flap dampening.
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp dampening dampened-paths
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1

   Network          From             Reuse    Path
*d 198.51.100.0/24  10.0.0.5         00:15:23 65002 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Show prefixes BGP learned but could NOT install in the main RIB (usually because a better-distance protocol — OSPF, EIGRP, static — already owns the prefix).
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp rib-failure
     Network            Next Hop            RIB-failure              RIB-NH Matches
     8.8.8.0/24         10.0.0.5            Higher admin distance     n/a
# Common cause: a static route with lower AD shadowing the BGP route.

Routing › BGP.desc: Inside a route-map: set the LOCAL_PREF on matched routes. Higher = more preferred. Local-pref stays inside your AS — used to influence OUTBOUND traffic decisions among iBGP peers.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-route-map)# set local-preference 200

Routing › BGP.desc: Inside a route-map: prepend extra AS numbers to the AS_PATH so the route looks longer / less attractive to neighbours. Used to influence INBOUND traffic engineering.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-route-map)# set as-path prepend 65001 65001 65001

Routing › BGP.desc: Inside a route-map: set the MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator). Tells an EXTERNAL neighbour which entry point into your AS to prefer. Lower = more preferred.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-route-map)# set metric 50

Routing › BGP.desc: Inside a route-map: tag the route with one or more communities. Special well-known values: no-export (do not send outside the AS), no-advertise (do not send to any peer), local-AS (do not send outside the local sub-AS).
Routing › BGP.example:
sw(config-route-map)# set community 65001:100 no-export
sw(config-route-map)# set community 65001:200 additive

Routing › BGP.desc: AS-confederation member-AS scheme — divide a large AS into sub-ASes that internally use eBGP but appear as the confederation-ID externally.
Routing › BGP.example:
sw(config-router)# bgp confederation identifier 65000
sw(config-router)# bgp confederation peers 65001 65002

Routing › BGP.desc: Tell the router which other sub-ASs belong to the same confederation as the local sub-AS.
Routing › BGP.example: sw(config-router)# bgp confederation peers 65001 65002 65003

Routing › BGP.desc: Display MPLS labels associated with BGP-learned prefixes (used in MPLS-VPN / 6PE / 6vPE deployments).
Routing › BGP.example:
router# show ip bgp labels
   Network          Next Hop      In label/Out label
   10.0.0.0/24      0.0.0.0       16003/aggregate(default)
   10.50.0.0/16     10.0.0.5      nolabel/24

Routing › BGP.desc: Filter BGP table by a literal AS-number anywhere in the path (matches across AS-set / private-AS removal).
Routing › BGP.example: sw# show ip bgp quote-regexp _65002_

Routing › BGP.desc: Filter the BGP table down to routes matching a predefined community-list.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config)# ip community-list 100 permit 65001:200
router# show ip bgp community-list 100
     Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
 *>  10.50.0.0/16     0.0.0.0                  0         32768 i
      Community: 65001:200

Routing › BGP.desc: Force the router to keep an UNFILTERED copy of every update received from a neighbour. Lets you run "show ip bgp neighbors X received-routes" and see what the peer is sending you BEFORE inbound policy. Memory-heavy on big tables.
Routing › BGP.example:
router(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5 soft-reconfiguration inbound
router# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include reconfig
  Inbound soft reconfiguration allowed

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Enables IS-IS process. The tag is a local name for the routing instance.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config)# router isis CORE
R1(config-router)#

What it means:
- "CORE" is a local instance name (not advertised) — same role as
  OSPF's process-id. Multiple IS-IS instances can run side-by-side.
- Without a tag, IOS uses the default "null" tag.
- The next required line under the process is "net <NSAP>".

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Sets the NET address (NSAP). Defines Area ID, System ID, and NSEL.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# net 49.0001.1111.1111.1111.00

R1# show clns | include NET
   NET: 49.0001.1111.1111.1111.00

What it means:
- 49        : AFI (Authority and Format Identifier) — 49 = private.
- 0001      : Area ID. Routers with the SAME area run L1 adjacencies.
- 1111.1111.1111 : 6-byte System ID (must be unique per router).
                   By convention: encode IPv4 loopback 1.1.1.1 as
                   0010.0100.0100 in BCD.
- 00        : NSEL (NSAP Selector) — must be 0x00 for an IS-IS router.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Activates IS-IS on an interface. Required for each interface to route IP.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# ip router isis CORE

R1# show isis interface Gi0/0
GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Routing Protocol: IS-IS (CORE)
    Circuit Type: level-1-2
    Interface number 0x1, local circuit ID 0x1
    Level-2 Metric: 10, Priority: 64

What it means:
- IS-IS is opt-in per-interface (no "network" statement like OSPF).
- The tag here MUST match the "router isis CORE" instance you want
  this interface to belong to.
- Without this command, IS-IS will form CLNS adjacencies but not
  carry any IP routes over them.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Sets the router role. Default is L1/L2. Use level-2-only for backbone routers.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# is-type level-2-only

R1# show isis | include type
   IS-Type: Level-2-only

What it means:
- level-1       : within-area only (like an OSPF stub).
- level-2-only  : backbone-only (like OSPF area 0).
- (default L1/L2): router belongs to both — talks L1 to in-area
                   neighbours and L2 to backbone neighbours.
- L1/L2 routers consume CPU/memory holding both LSDBs; restrict
  to level-2-only on pure transit / backbone routers.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Sets interface adjacency level. Prevents unnecessary hellos on specific links.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis circuit-type level-2

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include Circuit
  Circuit Type: level-2

What it means:
- Per-interface override of the global is-type.
- Useful when an L1/L2 router has some links that connect only to
  the backbone — restricting that interface to L2 stops it sending
  needless L1 hellos.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Verifies adjacencies. Displays neighbor System ID, State, and Circuit Type.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# show isis neighbors

System Id      Type Interface  IP Address       State Holdtime  Circuit Id
2222.2222.2222 L1   Gi0/0      192.168.1.2      UP    25        R2.01
3333.3333.3333 L2   Gi0/1      10.1.1.2         UP    27        R3.02

What it means:
- System Id   : 6-byte ID of the peer (or hostname if name-lookup is on).
- Type        : adjacency level (L1, L2, or L1L2).
- State       : UP / INIT / DOWN.
- Holdtime    : seconds before the adjacency is declared dead.
- Circuit Id  : DIS pseudonode identifier on broadcast links.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Sets interface cost. IS-IS defaults to 10 for all interfaces.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis metric 50

R1# show isis interface Gi0/0 | include Metric
  Level-1 Metric: 50, Priority: 64
  Level-2 Metric: 50, Priority: 64

What it means:
- Default metric is 10 — same on every interface regardless of
  bandwidth (unlike OSPF auto-cost).
- Without "metric-style wide" the maximum is 63 (narrow). With wide
  metrics the range is 1-16777215.
- Use "isis metric 50 level-2" to set per-level metrics.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Influences DIS election. Higher priority wins (Designated Intermediate System).
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis priority 100 level-2

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include DIS
  DR ID: R1.01, Priority: 100 (highest)

What it means:
- Range 0-127, default 64. Higher wins (opposite of OSPF tie-breaker
  on equal priority — IS-IS uses highest MAC, not lowest).
- DIS handles pseudonode LSP generation on broadcast LANs. Unlike
  OSPF DR, IS-IS supports preemption — a higher-priority router
  takes over immediately.
- Priority 0 still allows DIS election (use is-type adjustments to
  prevent it instead).

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Enables 24-bit metrics. Required for Traffic Engineering and large networks.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# metric-style wide

R1# show isis | include metric
   Metric Style: WIDE (TLV 22 / 135)

What it means:
- Narrow metric (default) is 6 bits (max 63 per link, max path 1023)
  and uses TLV 2/128 — too coarse for modern networks.
- Wide is 24 bits per link, max path 4,261,412,864. Required by
  MPLS-TE (TLV 22 carries TE attributes) and IPv6 (TLV 135).
- Set on EVERY router in the area or use "metric-style transition"
  for a phased migration.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Generates syslog messages when neighbors go up or down.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# log-adjacency-changes

%CLNS-5-ADJCHANGE: ISIS: Adjacency to 2222.2222.2222 (Gi0/0) Up, new adjacency
%CLNS-5-ADJCHANGE: ISIS: Adjacency to 2222.2222.2222 (Gi0/0) Down, hold time expired

What it means:
- Severity 5 syslog when adjacencies transition. Always enable on
  production for post-hoc flap analysis.
- Add "all" to also log L1↔L2 type changes.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Adjusts hello frequency. Controls how fast neighbors are discovered.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis hello-interval 3

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include Hello
  Hello (sec): 3, Multiplier: 3, Hold Time: 9

What it means:
- Default: 10 s. Lower for faster failure detection.
- Hold time = hello-interval × hello-multiplier (here 3 × 3 = 9 s).
- Combine with BFD if you need sub-second convergence — IS-IS hello
  bursts can drop on congested links.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Sets hold time (Hello Interval x Multiplier). Determines failure detection speed.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis hello-multiplier 3

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include Hold
  Hello (sec): 10, Multiplier: 3, Hold Time: 30

What it means:
- Hold time = hello-interval × multiplier.
- Default multiplier is 3, so default hold = 30 s for L1/L2 (and the
  DIS uses hello/3 for its own hellos).
- Increase to 5+ on flaky links to ride out brief packet loss without
  declaring the adjacency dead.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Maps System IDs to hostnames. Makes troubleshooting significantly easier.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# show isis hostname

Level  System ID         Hostname
L1     1111.1111.1111    R1 (local)
L1     2222.2222.2222    R2-Edge
L2     3333.3333.3333    R3-Core
*      4444.4444.4444    Unknown   ← hostname-lookup not on neighbour

What it means:
- The hostname TLV (137) is exchanged between IS-IS speakers so show
  output shows R2-Edge instead of 2222.2222.2222.
- Enable "hostname dynamic" globally so this router's hostname is
  also propagated.
- "Unknown" means the originating router has not enabled the feature.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Displays the LSDB. Shows all Link State Packets (LSPs) in the area.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# show isis database

IS-IS Level-1 Link State Database:
LSPID                LSP Seq Num  LSP Checksum  LSP Holdtime  ATT/P/OL
R1.00-00         *  0x00000003   0x4321        1198          0/0/0
R2.00-00            0x00000004   0x9876        1180          1/0/0
R2.01-00            0x00000001   0x1234        1180          0/0/0   ← pseudonode

What it means:
- LSPID R2.00-00 = LSP from R2's own node. R2.01-00 = pseudonode LSP
  for the broadcast LAN where R2 is DIS (.01 = circuit-id).
- Seq Num : monotonically increasing version. New > old wins.
- Holdtime: seconds until the LSP is purged (default 1200, refreshed
  every 900).
- ATT/P/OL: Attached / Partition / Overload bits.
- "*" marks LSPs originated by this router.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Enables cleartext authentication for interface adjacencies.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis password CISCO level-2

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include password
  Level-2 password: CISCO (cleartext)

What it means:
- Cleartext password — exchanged in IIH (IS-IS Hello) packets.
- Mismatched passwords cause the adjacency to stay in INIT state
  forever — but no syslog warning by default.
- Weak; prefer "isis authentication mode md5 + key-chain" for any
  non-lab use.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Authenticates Level 1 LSPs within a specific area.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# area-password SECRET

R1# show isis | include password
   Area authentication: SECRET (cleartext)

What it means:
- Authenticates Level-1 LSPs and SNPs (CSNP/PSNP) area-wide.
- Distinct from interface "isis password" (which protects IIHs only).
- Mismatch silently drops L1 LSPs from the offending neighbour —
  routes stop flowing while adjacency stays UP.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Authenticates Level 2 LSPs across the entire backbone.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# domain-password KEY

R1# show isis | include domain
   Domain authentication: KEY (cleartext)

What it means:
- Same role as area-password but for L2 (backbone) LSPs.
- Set identically on every L2-capable router across the IS-IS
  domain, otherwise backbone routes silently disappear.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Displays Layer 2 IS-IS details for an interface (SNPA, timers).
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# show clns interface Gi0/0

GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Checksums enabled, MTU 1497, Encapsulation SAP
  CLNS fast switching enabled
  Routing Protocol: IS-IS (CORE)
    Circuit Type: level-1-2
    Level-1 Designated IS: R1.01
    Next IS-IS LAN Level-1 Hello in 4 seconds
    Number of active level-1 adjacencies: 1
    SNPA: 000c.abcd.1234

What it means:
- SNPA (Subnetwork Point of Attachment) = the L2 MAC address used by
  IS-IS for the interface.
- "Designated IS: R1.01" = local router is DIS on this LAN.
- "Next Hello in 4 seconds" = countdown to the next outbound IIH.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Optimizes Ethernet links between two routers, skipping DIS election.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-if)# isis network point-to-point

R1# show clns interface Gi0/0 | include network
  Interface configuration: Point-to-point

What it means:
- Default for Ethernet is broadcast, which elects a DIS and uses
  pseudonode LSPs.
- p2p eliminates the DIS, halves the LSDB size for that link, and
  removes 30s of pseudonode-LSP timers from convergence.
- Both ends MUST agree — broadcast + p2p mismatch = no adjacency.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Summarizes routes at the L1/L2 border to reduce database size.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1-L1L2(config-router)# summary-address 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 level-2

R-backbone# show ip route isis | include 10.0.0.0
i L2 10.0.0.0/8 [115/20] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← single summary

What it means:
- Aggregates many L1 prefixes (10.x.x.x/24) into one L2 advertisement.
- A Null0 discard route is auto-installed locally to prevent loops
  for unallocated portions of the summary range.
- Default AD for IS-IS is 115; summary uses the same AD.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Imports external routes into the IS-IS database.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1(config-router)# redistribute static ip metric 10 level-2

R-neighbour# show ip route isis | include i.L2
i L2 192.0.2.0/24 [115/30] via 192.168.1.1, Gi0/0   ← redistributed static

What it means:
- "ip" suffix is required (different from OSPF/EIGRP) — IS-IS uses
  it to distinguish IPv4 from IPv6 / CLNS imports.
- Without level-2 the routes go into L1 by default. Most deployments
  redistribute into L2 only.
- Add "metric 10" so the import has a sane seed cost (default = 0
  in narrow, undefined in wide).

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Shows packet counters for hellos, LSPs, and CSNPs.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# show isis statistics

  IS-IS: Level-1 Hellos sent: 1240, received: 1238
  IS-IS: Level-2 Hellos sent: 620,  received: 620
  IS-IS: Level-1 LSPs originated: 4,  received: 8
  IS-IS: CSNPs sent: 240,  received: 240
  IS-IS: PSNPs sent: 12,   received: 14
  IS-IS: SPF runs (Level-1): 5,  (Level-2): 3
  IS-IS: Errors: corrupted LSPs: 0, auth fail: 0

What it means:
- Hello counters far apart (sent ≫ received) → unidirectional loss.
- High SPF count over short window → topology churn.
- Auth failures > 0 → password mismatch with a peer trying to come up.

Routing › IS-IS.desc: Restarts the IS-IS process and clears all adjacencies.
Routing › IS-IS.example:
R1# clear isis *

%CLNS-5-ADJCHANGE: ISIS: Adjacency to 2222.2222.2222 (Gi0/0) Down, manually cleared
%CLNS-5-ADJCHANGE: ISIS: Adjacency to 2222.2222.2222 (Gi0/0) Up, new adjacency

What it means:
- Bounces all IS-IS adjacencies and rebuilds the LSDB from scratch.
- Required to apply changes that don't auto-take effect (e.g. NET
  change, area-id change).
- Disruptive — prefer "clear isis adjacency <sysid>" for a single peer.
